Lee Hoytts Notice Of Motion And Motion To Enforce Settlement Agreement
Off Calendar Jan-08-2016 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES: Appearances: Andrew Zabronsky, for and with Lee Hoytt; Michael Friedman with Gregg McCullom, for Lisa Hoytt; Court Reporter Kimberly Jennings.

The Court noted that both cross-motions sought entry of judgment under section 664.4, and that both parties agreed that the Court interpret the settlement agreement and enter judgment thereon. The chief issue to be determined reates to $369,000 retained in escrow by lee Hoytt to repay estate taxes advanced by the trust. The settlement agreement provides for payment of estate taxes prior to distribution, and the question presented to the Court for determination is whether the advancement of the taxes by the trust should be reimbursed, or whether the obligation to pay taxes in the settlement agreement pertains only to taxes yet to be paid.
Mr. Freedman contended that the purpose of the settlement agreement was to resolve all disputes without going through the numerous costs and expenses of the estate. He stated that Lisa Hoytt and her counsel were unaware that the taxes were paid by the trust administrator and were expected to be reimbursed. He argued that the obligation for payment of taxes in the settlement agreement should be interpreted to apply only to taxes not yet paid at the time of the settlement, and as the settlement agreement was silent on the matter of reimbursement, the right to reimbursement had been waived and the $369,000 should be disbursed. He added that erroneous escrow instructions required payment of real estate taxes in advance by the estate which the tenants Longs and CVS were required to reimburse, and that provision should be made for Lisa Hoytt to get her share of such reimbursement by the tenants.
Mr. Zabronsky contended that the settlement agreement expressly provided that the estate taxes were an obligation to be paid prior to making the disbursement, and that no waiver had occurred. Both parties submitted the matter for determination by the Court.
The Court ruled that make judgment on the settlement agreement, holding that the $369,224 advanced estate taxes being held in escrow was to be reimbursed prior to the distibution. He further held that the bank accounts should be disbursed now, with interest, but disallowed prejudgment interest and recovery of attorneys fees. Additionally, the judgment shall include dismissal of the petition by Lisa Hoytt. At the Court's request, Mr. Zabronsky agreed to prepare an appropriate order and circulate it for approval as to form.
Judge: Peter J. Busch. Clerk: Craig Blackstone. Bailiff: Deputy Watts. Court Reporter: Marilyn Jennings, CSR #11452 (650) 218-9855.

The Court asked where we are in all of this. Mr. Zabronsky stated that they filed a status conference statement yesterday, that Mr. Hoytt had entered into an agreement to sell the center, that the parties agreed to amend the settlement agreement to allow for the property to sell at $18,000,000.00 if Mr. Hoytt made up the difference, that escrow closed on October 30, 2015, that $14,500,000.00 of the net sellers proceeds have languished in escrow because counsel for Lisa Hoytt contacted the title company stating that Lisa was a 50% member, represented that Lisa was a seller of the property, that they made false representations and instructed the title company regarding the disbursement, that they need an order from this court, that the disputes have now mushroomed, that they have repudiated the settlement agreement, that resolution is needed, that they want the court to set a quick deadline to file competing petitions to untangle the mess, that they want the court to assign blame and to award damages, that they need a briefing schedule and a hearing set to get the matter resolved, that Lisa has filed a motion and that they feel the petition route is better because of the scope of what can be accomplished. The Court asked if the parties have tried to talk and Mr. Freedman stated that they have. The Court stated that nothing prevents the filing of a motion or a petition and that until is sees what is in either it does not know the better way to resolve the issues. Mr. Zabronsky stated that there are 3 petitions before the court, Lisa's petition, Lee's petition for authority to sell which is now moot and the accounting petition filed by Lee which the settlement agreement provides for complete release. The Court stated that it is inclined to continue the hearing so that the parties can talk about the issues needing resolution and how the resolve them and that they can file whatever they think is appropriate. Mr. Freedman stated that there is an ex parte set for Thursday to shorten time as the motion should be heard at the earliest possible date. The Court stated that it is already being heard at the earliest possible date and clarified that the motion is set before Judge Busch. Mr. Freedman stated that the motion is set for December 16, 2015 and that this matter would need to be continued after that. The Court stated that they are to meet and confer immediately and that there will be a status hearing on the existing petitions in a couple of weeks to see a way forward that makes sense. Mr. Zabronsky suggested a one week continuance to file a brief explaining the routes. Mr. Freedman stated that 1 week is not enough time for him to respond. The Court stated that both parties always file things at the last minute with no time for the court to review, that it does not know how 2 siblings have totally screwed up the ability to get the deal done, that there is frustration in the court and that it is not going to jump through hoops. The 2 petitions for instructions and the accounting petition were continued to December 8, 2015 at 9:00 a.m., in Dept. 204 and the parties are told to try to work something out.

Mr. Freedman reported that he filed a declaration yesterday. Mr. Zabronsky reported that he also filed a declaration, that they were here on Tuesday, that there is a dispute as to whether the contract is still on, that his declaration includes an email from the buyer to Mr. Hoytt, that the buyer telephoned on Thursday and is mulling over the deal, wants to renegotiate, that while counsel cannot say the sale will probably close he can say that the contract is not terminated at this time and feels that the mootness doctrine does not apply. Mr. Freedman stated that the petition wants to modify the settlement agreement and that it says the PSA must be approved by the court. The Court stated that no clear cancellation is the argument here. Mr. Freedman responded that the evidence is fairly certain that it is cancelled, that the buyer wants to renegotiate and that would mean a new contract and that the petition was for a specific contract. The Court stated that we do not know that for sure right now, that the order would only approve the existing contract and that the question is whether the petitioner has a contract and wants approval of that contract prior to the deadline. Mr. Freedman stated that the main issue is that the petitioner asks for tremendous authority, that it circumvents the matter at hand, that the property is owned by the LLC and not the trust, that this is a significant modification of the settlement agreement, that there are tax issues and that the settlement agreement cannot be modified without written agreement. The Court stated that the settlement agreement did not specify where the sale price would come from. Mr. Freedman stated that the tax consequences depend on how the sale is reported, that they need an opportunity to deal with the tax consequences and that the parties agreed to sell the property at $19,000,000.00. Mr. Zabronsky stated that the petition goes not further than asking if the contract follows the settlement agreement, that they are not seeking modification, that the agreement calls for the sale at $19,000,000.00 to a third party buyer and that is what this is, that the provision is for Lee to rebate the buyer $750,000.00, that Lisa will receive half of the proceeds after the cost deduction, that the respondents never raised the issue of taxes in their opposition, that the reason for the petition is the rebate provision, that Lee has no intention of being the purchaser and that no provision of the settlement agreement is being violated. Mr. Freedman stated that their opposition mentioned that the $750,000.00 could be treated as income by the IRS. Off Calendar Aug-04-2015 Continued To Aug-11-15 At 09:00 A.M. In Dept 204
MINI MINUTES: Appearances: Andrew Zabronsky for Petitioner Lee Hoytt; Michael Freedman for Objector/beneficiary Lisa Hoytt

The Court stated that he had reviewed the Petitin and Objections, and that it doesn't appear that the Petition seeks to modify the settlement agreement or how the planned action harms Ms. Hoytt. Mr. Zabronsky reported that Continental Development Group (the potential buyer) is now holding off from closing escrow on the sale, but has not as yet terminated the sale. He stated that should the buyer withdraw his offer, there were two other potential buyers at the $18+ million purchase price who could step in to complete the transaction. The Court inquired whether the withdrawal of the current offer would make the Petition moot, as only that transaction is before the Court. Mr. Zabronsky responded that since the same issues would arise with the alternative buyers, that even if the prospective buyer ultimately chooses to terminate the sale, the decision of the Court would be helpful in any such alternative sale. The Court stated that the only matter before it is the proposed sale to Continental, and that it is not the function of the Court to give merely advisory rulings or rulings based on speculated circumstances. Mr. Freedman opined that the Petition is moot, as the buyer will be terminating the sale. He stated that the buyer has sent a letter stating that the property value is under $14 million. He further stated that the other prospective sales were also dead, as the buyers would not be willing to pay a purchase price of $18-$19 million as the property is not worth it. Mr. Zabronsky responded that the position taken by the buyer was merely an attempt to make the purchase at a lower price, and that as there has been no termination of the sale, the issues raised by the Petition are not moot.
The matter was continued by the Court for one week to August 11, 2015 at 9:00 a.m., in Dept. 204, to see if the matter resolves in some way. He stated that any supplemental submission to the Court regarding the Petition should be filed and delivered to Department 204 by August 7, 2015, for the Court to have the opportunity to review it prior to that hearing.

MINI MINUTES: Appearances: Michael Freedman for and with Lisa Hoytt. Susan Von Herrmann and Caitlin Carey for Lee Hoytt (Respondent/Turstee).

Mr. Freedman stated that they agreed to mediate, that they selected a Mediator and mediation date of February 11, 2014, but that they have some issues to address with regard to the accounting prior to getting to mediation. Mr. Freedman also stated that he received the accounting, that he is reviewing it and working on main discrepancies with the accounting, spoke of distributions made by the Trustee, raised the issue of income from the Plaza being used to pay for expenses of the Marital Trust, the issue of co-mingling of funds and stated that Court supervision of the accounting is needed so that it is done properly. The Petition for Instructions was continued to March 19, 2014 at 9:00 a.m., in Dept. 204. Status on filing of the accounting is set for February 19, 2014 at 9:00 a.m., in Dept. 204. The accounting petition is to be filed by February 7, 2014 with a hearing date of March 19, 2014.

Judge: Mary E. Wiss. Clerk: Rosie Noguera. Bailiff: Deputy Allen. (Not Reported)
